result:
The rain hammered against the frosted glass of the watchmaker shop. Elias sat hunched over his scarred wooden workbench, his magnifier pressed firmly into his right eye. Dust motes danced in the pale light of a single flickering bulb. He loved the steady rhythm of ticking clocks. They were predictable. They made sense. Tonight, however, the rhythm was broken. A strange piece of metal lay before him on a velvet cloth. It was a gear, perfectly round, yet etched with symbols he had never seen in his seventy years. The metal felt warm. It hummed against his deeply wrinkled skin.

He picked up the tiny object with silver tweezers. It was neither brass nor steel. The surface shifted like oil on water, reflecting colors that seemed to bleed into the shadows of the room. Where had it come from? A mysterious man in a dark coat had left it earlier that afternoon, claiming it belonged to a timepiece that could fix broken promises. Elias had laughed, assuming the man was a wandering eccentric. Now, alone in the dead of night, the joke felt incredibly hollow. Elias grabbed a disassembled old pocket watch and decided to test the impossible little gear.

He slotted the gear into the heart of the mechanism. Surprisingly, it fit with absolute perfection, as if the watch had been waiting for this exact piece for a century. He wound the mainspring. A sharp click echoed through the shop. Then, the hands began to spin. They did not move forward. The second hand ticked backward, moving with impossible speed. The hour and minute hands followed, blurring into a steady silver circle. The grandfather clocks lining the walls suddenly chimed in unison, their heavy brass pendulums swinging wildly out of rhythm. The air in the room grew noticeably cold.

Elias gasped and dropped the pocket watch onto the desk. It did not stop spinning. Instead, the ambient noise of the rain outside began to change. The drops sounded like they were rising from the puddles on the street and flying up into the dark clouds. A spilled cup of cold coffee on his desk suddenly leaped back into his ceramic mug, perfectly warm and completely undisturbed. Elias pushed his chair back, his heart hammering against his ribs. He watched as a dead moth on the windowsill twitched, fluttered its gray wings, and flew directly out the nearby open window.

He was moving backward through time. The realization hit him with the force of a speeding train. If he let the watch run, where would it take him? Yesterday? Last week? Last year? His mind immediately raced to a singular, agonizing memory. Ten years ago, his beloved wife, Clara, had stepped out into a terrible winter storm to buy groceries. She had slipped on the frozen pavement and never woke up. If he just allowed this magical device to keep winding backward, he could reach that snowy morning. He could lock the front door. He could finally save her life.

He stared at the dial. The blur of the hands was mesmerizing. He felt his own body growing lighter, the familiar aches in his joints suddenly fading away. His arthritis vanished. His vision sharpened. The gray hairs falling onto
